coredata.dc:title: Economical, one-pot, and green synthesis of plant-based carbon quantum dots for efficient visible-light photocatalytic dye degradation and water purification

coredata.dc:creator: Mirzaeifard, Zahra
Shariatinia, Zahra
coredata.dc:description:
Background Green, in-situ, one-pot, facile, and economical synthesis of efficient photocatalysts to destroy emerging aqueous contaminants is essential.
Method Biocompatible carbon quantum dots (CQDs) photocatalysts were successfully synthesized from a plant source called garden thyme as a low-cost precursor by one-step calcination method. CQDs were synthesized in both pure form and co-doped with fluorine and boron indicating size of <10 nm and favorable quantum yield of 40%. Four key factors including synthesis temperature, synthesis time, fluorine and boron weight ratios, and CQDs size were investigated on removal rates of organic pollutant Rhodamine B (RhB).
Significant findings 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs with synthesized at 400 ºC for 5 h with a size of <10 nm, showed the highest photocatalytic RhB degradation performances of 86.1% and 89% within 40 and 60 min, respectively. This improved performance was originated from synergetic influences of quantum size, co-doping, promising quantum yield, and great visible light harvesting, that satisfactorily separated and transported light-created electron-hole pair. Trapping experimental data approved that hydroxyl radicals were dominant reactive species that oxidized RhB on F,B co-doped CQDs catalysts upon visible light radiation. Overall, CQDs could be easily synthesized as a green and super-cheap photocatalyst and would be effective for wastewater treatments.

head.ce:title: Economical, one-pot, and green synthesis of plant-based carbon quantum dots for efficient visible-light photocatalytic dye degradation and water purification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), P.O.Box:15875-4413, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), P.O.Box:15875-4413, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green, in-situ, one-pot, facile, and economical synthesis of efficient photocatalysts to destroy emerging aqueous contaminants is essential.

Biocompatible carbon quantum dots (CQDs) photocatalysts were successfully synthesized from a plant source called garden thyme as a low-cost precursor by one-step calcination method. CQDs were synthesized in both pure form and co-doped with fluorine and boron indicating size of <10 nm and favorable quantum yield of 40%. Four key factors including synthesis temperature, synthesis time, fluorine and boron weight ratios, and CQDs size were investigated on removal rates of organic pollutant Rhodamine B (RhB).

10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs with synthesized at 400 ºC for 5 h with a size of <10 nm, showed the highest photocatalytic RhB degradation performances of 86.1% and 89% within 40 and 60 min, respectively. This improved performance was originated from synergetic influences of quantum size, co-doping, promising quantum yield, and great visible light harvesting, that satisfactorily separated and transported light-created electron-hole pair. Trapping experimental data approved that hydroxyl radicals were dominant reactive species that oxidized RhB on F,B co-doped CQDs catalysts upon visible light radiation. Overall, CQDs could be easily synthesized as a green and super-cheap photocatalyst and would be effective for wastewater treatments.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial development and quick population growth have led to huge environmental pollution as one of the greatest challenges in the past few decades [1]. More than 700,000 tons of dyes are estimated to be synthesized yearly, and more than 10,000 types are currently used in industries [2]. Organic dyes are employed by textile, printing, and plastics industries and damage aquatic ecosystems by inhibiting light transmission and interfering with photosynthesis [3]. Furthermore, they can cause critical health problems along with cancer, and birth defects, in addition to hormone imbalances if ingested [4,5]. The direct conversion of solar power to chemical energy can be exploited to deal with environmental disasters in the future [6].

Recently, photocatalytic semiconductor technology has received considerable attention in organic pollution removal, bacterial disinfection and power generation because of its benign by-products, strong photocatalytic ability, and economic and environmental advantages [7,8]. The ultraviolet (UV) light driven photocatalytic H2O splitting by TiO2 electrode was initiated in 1972 by Fujishima and Honda [9]. Researchers examined photocatalytic pollutants degradation by the use of semiconductor powders like TiO2, SiC, ZnO, and CQDs. The UV/visible light active photocatalysts consist of TiO2, Fe2O3, ZnO, CQDs, Bi2WO6, BiVo4, Ta2O5, Ta3N5, TaON, and more others [10,11]. The practical developments of traditional photocatalysts (ZnO, SnO2, and TiO2) have been constrained through their low solar-power usage efficiency, wide band-gap, and excessive recombination rate of e − and h + [12-14]. These materials are required to have characteristics consisting of strong light absorption ability, appropriate band gap, and redox potential [15-18]. Also, they have to be stable, non-toxic, inexpensive, and effortlessly machinable into desired shapes [19]. Despite the fact that the application of photocatalysts is restrained by problems including slow response time and low light absorption intensity, an adsorption decomposition system based on adsorbent-photocatalytic materials can excellently improve removal efficiency for the treatment of polluted water [20,21].

Nowadays, CQDs have demonstrated fascinating physical and chemical properties [22]. Published articles exhibit effectiveness of photocatalysts based on CQD for degrading pollutants, synthesis of organic materials, CO2 reduction, and H2 generation. CQDs contain carbon atoms with sp3 and sp2 hybridizations, numerous functionalities, and tunable photoluminescence dependent on their own compositions [23]. Based on specific structural features, CQDs were divided to carbon nanodots and graphene QDs. The former includes one or some graphene layers, in which horizontal dimension is extensively longer compared with height. Carbon nanodots usually reveal spherical shapes that are typically called CQDs due to they possess an apparent crystal lattice. CQDs was first found upon purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes with the aid of gel electrophoresis in 2004 [24]. After that, photocatalysts based on CQD were extensively used by researchers [25]. CQDs photocatalytic performance is associated with their inherent optical properties. CQDs display emission at low wavelengths (blue region) because of sp2 bonds within their sp3 networks. Also, CQDs were synthesized to show emissions at longer wavelengths (near-infrared and yellow regions) with the usage of unique starting materials, heteroatom-doping, specific surface functional groups, and better charges’ separation which could be supported by density functional theory calculations [26].

Synthetic techniques toward CQDs are categorized to top-down and bottom-up methods. Top-down techniques consist of arc-discharge, electrochemical etching, and laser ablation, perform physical exfoliation of massive carbon materials (carbon nanotubes, graphite) to highly crystalline CQDs displaying smooth surface [27]. Bottom-up methods are alkali/acid-assisted ultrasonic, solvothermal, nitric acid oxidation, and thermal pyrolysis oxidation [27]. Due to their nonmetallic nature, less than 10 nm in size, CQDs have unique photophysical and chemical properties such as broad light absorption, favorable photoluminescence, electron transport, excellent photostability, high aqueous solubility, up-conversion, photosensitization chemical inertness, facile functionalization, and low toxicity when compared to other allotropes of carbon [28,29]. CQDs display structural and optical characteristics which make them outstanding photocatalysts including excellent sun light absorption, effectual redox reactions, and rapid charge carriers’ transfer. Furthermore, CQDs can be exploited in photoluminescence quenching through a mixture of numerous materials, which include ions, molecules, conjugated compounds, and semiconductors [30].

Graphene QDs exhibit photoluminescence properties tuned from near-infrared to far UV through tuning morphology, particle diameter, functional groups, band structure, doping, and defects [30]. Besides, CQDs may show up-conversion photoluminescence, that is notably high-quality for bio-imaging and efficient visible-light-responsive photocatalysts. Therefore, CQDs are considered next generation of green nanomaterials contributing to countless promising areas like photoelectrocatalysis, targeted medicine delivery, photovoltaic devices, imaging and bio-imaging. The structures of CQDs may be either amorphous, crystalline or semi-crystalline. Crystalline CQDs are generally derived from graphene through top-down artificial strategies and are denoted as graphene quantum dots [23]. Moreover, CQDs with crystalline graphitic centers have been acquired through bottom-up artificial strategies [27]. Notably, different studies supported the name CQDs [31]. The CQDs term is used for amorphous CQDs but another phrase is carbon nanodots for amorphous or semi-crystalline structures [28].

The CQDs can serve as photocatalysts, photosensitizers, electron acceptors/mediators, and up-conversion materials [1]. Photocatalysis is initiated by ultraviolet, visible or infrared radiation absorption by the photocatalyst that leads to the chemical transformation of reactants [32]. CQDs can harvest light through direct absorption. The absorbed photons excite electrons from highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) into lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) by creating holes within HOMO. Produced electrons and holes in CQDs photocatalysts then result in the chemical transformation of reactants [32].

CQDs have a high light absorption capacity within UV−visible (UV–Vis) area because of π-π* transitions in the sp2 C = C double bond presenting in the inner core [33]. The CQDs surface includes numerous groups (e.g. -COOH, -OH, -NH2) relying on the synthetic route. These groups can absorb the excitation light wavelengths and most usually their absorption takes place inside the visible range because of n–π*. The CQDs benefit from their ultra-small size, aqueous solubility, and simplicity in their synthesis. Heteroatom doping introduces n→π* transitions corresponding to C = X (X = S, N, O) bonds and creates doping states near CQDs LUMO levels [34]. Hybridization of O 2p and π orbitals happens in oxygen functionalized CQDs (COOH and OH groups) that produces states upper than HOMO in CQDs. Hence, doping powerfully modifies energy levels in semiconductor CQDs and boost their photocatalytic performance [35].

Numerous techniques are being used today, including non-metal doping (such as boron, nitrogen, sulfur, oxygen, phosphorus) and metal doping to modify physical and chemical properties of materials [36,37]. Doping, especially anion doping, is a cost-effective way to boost visible light photocatalytic capacity in carbon by expanding light harvesting range towards visible area. In theory, doped material exhibits a somewhat smaller bandgap and weak absorption tail within visible area as midgap levels are created among bandgap due to the integration of a dopant inside the carbon framework [38]. Particularly, doping CQDs with B atoms effectively controls the optical properties because the B atom's vacant p-orbital may effectively conjugate with C = C atoms, reducing the space between B3+ and the sp2 bonds of B-CQDs and improving the electron delocalization inside the carbon matrix [39]. Consequently, quantum yield increases as a result of the enhanced radiative recombination, which displays good selectivity and responsiveness for reactants. On the other hand, very electronegative fluorine boosts the CQDs photoluminescence. Additionally, the biocompatibility, cellular uptake, and stability of F-doped CQDs are favorable. Few studies have yet been reported on F-doped CQDs [40].

RhB belongs to the class of xanthene dyes and is widely used in industry due to its good solubility, remarkable color intensity, and high stability. RhB is a reddish-purple powder with a molecular formula of C28H31N2O3Cl and molecular weight of 479 g/mol, which is considered a carcinogenic dye [41]. Given its wide application in various industries that release processed waste into the environment, it is unsurprising that RhB is found in the aquatic environment. However, water pollution by RhB is associated with several environmental and health problems for humans and animals. Although the concentration of RhB in water is very low (∼1.0 mg/L), it gives the water a distinct color, making it unsuitable for domestic and food use. Removing the color of RhB dye from wastewater using conventional water treatment techniques usually involves challenging processes because RhB is a highly soluble and persistent dye found in wastewater. Though efficient physiochemical methods have been applied, limitations associated with these technologies, such as production of secondary wastes in chemical precipitation process, cannot be ignored. Many methods have been developed to eliminate wastewater, but there are limitations. Among them, photocatalytic reduction is more advantageous because photocatalysis can convert dye molecules into less toxic or non-toxic ones without generating secondary waste. Hence, photocatalytic methods based on advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), which rely heavily on reactive oxygen species such as hydroxyl and superoxide radicals as strong oxidants, can remove textile dyes from wastewater. Therefore, photocatalytic processes are very effective in removal of RhB from water [41].

Herein, green, in-situ, one-pot, and facile synthesis of a novel biomass CQDs photocatalyst was done based on garden thyme plant using the calcination method. The garden thyme is a native plant in Iran that is easily accessible and also very cheap. To find the optimum synthesis parameters, different temperatures (300, 400, and 500 °C) for diverse periods (3, 4, 5, and 10 h) were performed. Besides, various weight percents of F and B elements (1%−1%, 5%−5%, 7.5%−7.5%, 10%−10%, 12.5%−12.5% and 15%−15%) were co-dopinged performed. The synthesized photocatalysts were potentially suitable for the degradation of textile organic pollutant dyes as they were subjected to photocatalytic RhB degradation under visible light radiation, which is clearly shown in Fig. 1 along with the RhB degradation rates upon 180 min of visible light irradiation. The physicochemical properties of CQDs were investigated through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), elemental mapping images, energy-dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS), photoluminescence (PL) spectrum, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS), Mott-Schottky, and photocurrents responses. Also, historical experimental design was carried out on to examine effects of four important parameters varied in the reactions on the photocatalytic degradation performances. Lastly, it was verified that 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs was the champion green photocatalyst which has a high potential for upcoming water treatments.

Studies have shown that the doping of heteroatoms in the structure of semiconductors improves the physical and chemical properties and thus improves the photocatalytic properties because heteroatoms cause structural defects [42]. Also, doping with two or more types of heteroatoms can further improve the effects that are dependent on the contents of heteroatoms and related to the impact of synergistic coupling between heteroatoms. Therefore, in order to improve the photocatalytic activity of CQDs, simultaneous doping of two F and B heteroatoms was done because F was an electron-rich element, which has more electrons than carbon, and B is an electron-deficient element, which has one less electron than carbon. The two F and B elements can create local n-type and p-type sites within the F,B co-doped CQDs and modify the optical, electrical, physical, and chemical properties of CQDs as the photocatalysts.

Garden thyme was purchased from an herbal medicine company in Iran. Other materials were obtained from Merck Corporation, including boric acid (H3BO3), ammonium hydrogen fluoride (NH4HF), RhB (>98%), benzoquinone (BQ, >99%), methanol (>99%), and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA, >99%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pure and f,b co-doped CQDs photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of CQDs co-doped with F and B was done from two substances, H3BO3 and NH4HF with weight percentages of 1%−1%, 5%−5%, 7.5%−7.5%, 10%−10%, 12.5%−12.5%, and 15%−15%. In each step, as Fig. 2 a shows, one of the above-mentioned amounts of H3BO3 and NH4HF was added to chopped garden thyme and mixed with 25 mL of distilled water and then heated for almost an hour to vaporize its extract. The heater temperature was slowly increased from 50 °C to 200 °C. When water was completely evaporated and the thyme became a paste, it was transferred to the furnace and subjected to temperatures of 300, 400 and 500 ºC for 3, 4, and 5 and 10 h. Of course, the thyme that was subjected to a temperature of 500 ºC could not withstand more than 5 h and turned into ash. Next, after the specified period of time, they were taken out of the oven, cooled down, and grounded in a mortar to obtain fine powders, which were examined for photocatalytic activity. Finally, the CQDs co-doped with F and B with a weight percentage of 10%−10%, which was prepared under a temperature of 400 °C for 5 h, revealed supreme photocatalytic property and hence it was selected as optimum photocatalyst. For the synthesis of pure CQDs, similar to the above process, garden thyme was mixed with 25 mL of distilled water, heated, transferred to the furnace, and calcined under a temperature of 400 ºC for a duration of 5 h.

Crystallinity of samples was investigated using XRD (INEL EQUINOX 3000) via Cu kα irradiation (λ=0.15,406 nm). HR-TEM (EM900, Zeiss, Germany) and FESEM (Zeiss, Germany) micrographs displayed particle diameters and morphology of CQDs. EDS (ZEISS, SIGMA) spectroscopy and elemental mapping images afforded the chemical composition and element distribution in CQDs. FTIR spectra (Equinox 55 Bruker) were taken within 400−4000 cm−1 wavenumber area. PL spectra (Varian Carry Eclips) were recorded using excitation at 280 nm. UV−Vis DRS spectra were provided by Avantes-Avaspec-2048 spectrometer. Using Kubelka−Munk formula (Eq. (1)), optical band gaps were measured [43], where h, ν, α, and A respectively reveal Planck constant, frequency, light absorption coefficient, transition probability constant. The n-value changes by optical transition type in semiconductor materials and it is 1/2 or 2 for direct (carbon nanomaterials) or 2 for indirect transitions, respectively. Plotting (αhν)2 versus hυ gave Eg by extrapolation towards horizontal axis.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) based on Al Kα irradiation (1486 eV) and resolution of 0.1% was used to determine the chemical bonding states of all surface elements existing within up to 10 nm depth. The XPS results were deconvoluted by CasaXPS software (version 2.3.16 PR1.6). EIS spectra, Mott-Schottky, and transient photocurrent response analyses were obtained using an electrochemical three-electrode cell. EIS spectra were acquired within 0.001–1 MHz frequency range with low AC voltage (5 V). Three working, counter, and reference electrodes were photocatalyst, Pt, and Ag/AgCl, respectively. In addition, a Xe lamp (100 W) and +0.1 V bias voltage were used to achieve photocurrent response of photocatalyst.

Photocatalytic RhB degradation activities were estimated using pure and F,B co-doped CQDs under visible light radiation. For this purpose, photocatalyst was dispersed within 200 mL of RhB aqueous solution. In photocatalytic degradation processes carried out by visible-light irradiation in a handmade cylindrical reactor, the photocatalytic characteristics of pure and F,B co-doped CQDs were examined. The Pyrex reactor had an inner and outer diameter of 8 and 10 cm, respectively, with a capacity of 500 mL. To stop light from leaking into the surrounding area, aluminum foil wrapped the reactor's outside wall. Cold water was flowed between two walls surrounding glass reactor so that reaction temperature was maintained constant. As radiation source, a halogen lamp with UV-stop (λ>400 nm) was positioned in reactor's upper center. The cylindrical reactor was utilized in this investigation. Before turning on the lamp, reaction mixture was first left under the dark for 1 h to make sure it had reached equilibrium adsorption. Photocatalytic degradation reaction then occurred after turning on the lamp. Subsequently, CQDs catalysts were separated from reaction through centrifuging for 30 min at 10,000 rpm and then 3 mL of reaction mixture was sampled every 30 min to assess photocatalytic RhB degradation performance (De) according to UV–Vis spectra in 200–800 nm spectral range. Eq. (2) was used to obtain De values, where A0 and A, respectively, show initial and ultimate absorbance while C and C0 exhibit RhB concentrations at times t and 0. Eq. (3) afforded first-order rate constant (k) of photocatalytic reaction.

Herein, CQDs synthesis was performed in a simple, one-step, in-situe, and green process based on an ecological strategy using garden thyme as a new plant source, which has not yet been used and as far as we know, it is firstly applied in this work. Variuos temperatures and reaction times were applied for the CQDs synthesis from thyme and it was found out that the CQDs synthesized at 400 °C for 5 h had the optimal photocatalytic performance. Moreover, to boost the photocatalytic RhB degradation efficiency, the CQDs were co-doped using two elements, boron and fluorine, which significantly increased the photocatalytic activity. In the following sections, physical, chemical, and photocatalytic characteristics of pure and F,B co-doped CQDs photocatalysts, experimental design, trapping experimental results, and reaction mechanism will be presented.

It is worth mentioning that in practical photocatalytic wastewater treatments, CQDs should be immobilized on solid matrices in order to make them commercially viable when using slurry photocatalytic systems. However, it is notable that there are other photocatalytic reactors such as fixed-bed reactors, in which the non-supported photocatalysts can be used in the desired photocatalytic reactions (such as pollutant degradation and water purification processes). In this method, the CQDs immobilization on solid matrices is not an obligation and high-performance CQDs photocatalysts (including doped CQDs) of high activity and stability can be effectively used in practical wastewater treatment processes.

The advantages of the CQDs photocatalysts developed in this work can be explained by the fact that they are obtained from a natural source, which display very high activity and stability in photocatalytic RhB degradation without the need for using any co-catalysts, promoters, and supports. Within 60 min, the champion photocatalyst 10%F-10%B co-doped s CQDs ample degraded 89% of the organic pollutant RhB. In similar articles, carbon is usually used to create two- or three-component composites. For example, CQDs are combined with metals, metal oxides, metal sulfides, carbon nitride, perovskite/spinel compounds (especially bismuth-based semiconductors, iron-containing catalysts, etc.) to improve their photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs is a very cost-effective photocatalyst, which is not only highly active in photocatalytic degradation of water contaminant but also it demonstrates very good reusability and stability.

Fig. 2b indicates XRD patterns for un-doped and F,B co-doped CQDs. In all samples, a broad diffraction peak is observed around 26.66˚, which is consistent with the (002) hkl plane of carbon-based materials (JCPDS card no. 26–1076) [44]. In addition, small peaks are detected on the amorphous carbon peak, which are due to the use of natural garden thyme as the plant-based carbon sources. As a result, these small peaks are related to the impurities caused by the carbon source. Besides, two sharp peaks are located at 28º and 47º that are the characteristic peaks of crystalline B2O3 (JCPDS card No. 06–0297) at 28º assigned to the (310) hkl plane [45]. Therefore, both amorphous and crystalline phases are present in the synthesized CQDs framework.

After doping with B and F elements, not only the interfacial spacing increases from 0.334 nm to 0.335 nm due to various amounts of B,F added (from 1 to 15 wt%), but also the intensity of almost all peaks gradually increases. This result indicates that B and F heteroatoms can be incorporated into the C crystal structure, leading to an increase in the interfacial stacking distance, and also a gradual sharpening of the amorphous carbon peak [46]. According to EDS analysis, the natural garden thyme plant used in this synthesis as the carbon source contain diverse metals. Hence, it seems that some peaks are created (e.g., near 30, 55, and 70º) due to the formation of metal oxides.

The chemical structures of pure and F,B co-doped CQDs were studied using FTIR analysis between 500 and 4000 cm−1 region (Fig. 3 a). It is found that all CQDs exhibit two main bands at 3430 and 1630 cm−1 which are respectively correlated to OH vibration and C = O (stretching). Bands at 2925 and 2857 cm−1 reveals asymmetric vibrations of CH2 and CH3 bonds [47].

Absorption peaks around 2212 and 2302 cm−1 are related to the asymmetric stretching band in carbon dioxide. In pure carbon, the absorption peak related to carbonyl is observed at 1630 cm−1, while the band at about 1624 cm−1 exhibits stretching vibrations of conjugated C = O and C = C bonds presenting within co-doped CQDs. Band at 1390 cm−1 confirms cyclic carbon moieties exist in the backbone. Additionally, C = C stretching vibration occurs between 1400–1500 cm−1 [48]. In this spectrum, it can be seen that there are C = O (in carboxylic acid moieties) stretching vibrations at 1711 cm−1, CO-C (epoxy) at 1261 cm−1, and CO-H (alkoxy) at 1026 cm−1 [49,50].

Two new absorption bands appear in the F,B co-doped samples, the first one is the band at 1060 cm−1 indicating semi-ionic character of the C-F bonds. Analogous spectra were obtained for fluoride containing graphite materials [51]. The second band in F,B co-doped samples is detected at about 1840 cm−1 that correspond to C(O)F moiety [52]. As there are also Si, S, and N elements in the CQDs framework (proved by EDS spectra in following sections), the C-B, C-Si, C-S, and CN bonds can be seen about 1000 cm−1 [53]. Overall, FT-IR spectra confirm synthesis of graphitic CQDs with plentiful C = O, C(O)OH, C-F, C-B, and OH bonds.

The PL and absorbance spectra were acquired for pure and F,B co-doped CQDs to explore their optical characteristics, see Figs. 3b and 4 a. Using PL spectra, one can study electron-hole separation and charge transport in various materials [54]. Herein, based on PL spectral emission data, we would like to find if simultaneously doping of B and F elements in CQDs decreases electron-hole recombination because PL peaks appear upon electron-hole recombination in semiconductors. Fig. 3b demonstrates room temperature PL spectra for pure and F,B co-doped CQDs. A PL peak near 560 nm in pure CQDs is detected due to direct band to band transition by electron-hole recombination within bandgap. Also, the 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs demonstrate the lowermost emission intensity among all samples, establishing the least electron-hole recombination under visible light irradiation. Thus, several mid-gap energy levels created by F and B doping into CQDs are not recombination sites for the pairs but they separate electron and hole and diminish electron-hole recombination [55]. Therefore, simultaneous doping of fluorine and boron is very beneficial as it favorably separates charge carriers, declines electron-hole recombination, and also improves electron transport.

Suitable photocatalysts have the ability of effective solar energy absorption extend towards visible area. Hence, absorbance spectra were attained to evaluate optical features of un-doped CQDs and six F,B co-doped CQDs with different contents of fluorine and boron. It can be seen from Fig. 4a that all samples show absorbance edges in the visible light region. Pure carbon indicates an absorption edge at about 550 nm. This absorption originates from the intrinsic band gap absorption by electron transport from valence band towards conduction band.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FE-SEM/HR-TEM micrographs, EDS, and elemental mapping
ce:section.ce:para: To further study the internal structure of 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs and to confirm the quantum dot formation, the HR-TEM images are shown in Fig. 6b. The particle shapes are shown to be almost spherical with diameters of <10 nm, as displayed by circles. The lattice space of 0.21 nm ca be related to (002) plane in CQDs. These results approve that the carbon dots are successfully synthesized (see Fig. 6c).

In fact, HR-TEM images were used as more evidence to prove the existence of CQDs. This is because FE-SEM micrographs in Fig. 5 cannot exhibit the quantum dot size (particle diameter less than 10 nm) of the synthesized pure and F,B co-doped carbon quantum dots. This is related to strong particles agglomeration because of their extremely small quantum dot sizes and the reason behind this observation is that quantum dots highly tend to stick together on the sample holder used for taking the FE-SEM images. The method used for the deposition of CQDs onto the sample holder causes the CQDs to be stacked on each other and look like a film surface or large aggregates in the resulting FE-SEM micrographs. However, the quantum dot sizes of the synthesized pure and F,B co-doped CQDs (<10 nm) are proven using HR-TEM images provided in Figs. 6b and 6c.

It should be noted that the presence of elements C, Ca, K, O, Mg, P, S, Cl, Si, N, Al, and Na in pure carbon dot is determined based on EDS spectrum (Fig. S2), which indicates different elements exist in natural thyme plant source. Also, C, O, K, Ca, S, P, Mg, F, and B elements are identified in 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs, confirming the successful doping of boron (2.2%) and fluorine (7.3%), see Fig. S3. The distributions of elements in the pure and 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs photocatalysts are analyzed by the mapping images presented in Figs. S2-S3.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further carried out to determine the doping levels and chemical bonds in B and F doped CQDs sample. The XPS survey scan confirms the presence of carbon (C 1 s), nitrogen (N 1 s), oxygen (O 1 s), boron (B 1 s), magnesium (Mg 2 s), fluorine (F 1 s), calcium (Ca 2p 3/2), phosphorus (P 2p), and potassium (K 2 s) elements with their distinct peaks at around 284, 398, 530, 191, 88, 684, 346, 132, and 376 eV, respectively (Fig. 7 a), which are completely consistent with the EDS results. Due to it is obtained using the natural source (garden thyme), CQDs has some impurities from 1 to 14.74%. In other words, the presence of fluorine and boron was confirmed by XPS. As can be seen, in the XPS analysis, the presence of a nitrogen atom has been detected, which was not detected in the EDS results, which is due to the fact that the location of the nitrogen peak is very close to the location of the oxygen and fluorine peaks, and as a result, the peaks overlapped in EDS.

The detailed bindings of all elements can be analyzed in their related high-resolution spectra, as shown in Figs. 7b-7j. It can be seen in Fig. 7b that the C 1 s spectrum in F,B co-doped CQDs is deconvoluted into four peaks: The first peak at 284.7 eV corresponds to carbon atom bonded only to other carbon atoms or hydrogen atoms (CC, C = C, or CH bonds). The second peak at 285.4 eV represents carbon atom bound to one or two non-carbonyl oxygen atoms, i.e. CO, CN, and C-P bonds. The third peak at 287.5 eV exhibits bonding of carbon to carbonyl group (C = O) and finally, the fourth peak at 289.5 eV is attributed to the COOH bond that overlaps with the peak related to C-F covalent bond. The main carbon signal (sp3 C) indicates amorphous carbon and other peaks correspond to carbon-fluorine and carbon-oxygen bonds. These oxygen-containing functional groups are created by oxygen binding to carbon during direct calcination process [57].

In Fig. 7c, the O 1 s spectrum is deconvoluted into four main peaks at 530.1, 531.1, 532.7, and 533.6 eV The peak observed at 530.1 eV can be ascribed to hydroxyl (OH) groups on the surface. The binding energy of OC = O and B-O bonds appear at 531.1 eV, while CO bond is observed at around 532.7 eV, and the last peak at 533.6 eV is related to the C = O bond [31,58]. The N 1 s spectrum has been deconvoluted into three peaks which can be assigned to CNH at 398.4 eV, N substituted in graphite layer, which is known as graphite N, in the binding energy range of 400–401 eV, and N-oxide at 402.6 eV are observed in Fig. 7d [59].

The presence of fluorine in the synthesized CQDs sample can be confirmed by F 1 s XPS spectrum which can be seen in Fig. 7e. The peaks related to F bonds in CQDs are assigned to covalent and semi-ionic C-F bonds. Three peaks are observed including a sharp peak from 684 to 685 eV and two broad peaks from 685 to 690 eV A high intensity F 1 s signal is observed at 684.1 eV, which is attributed to the semi-ionic C-F bond and two other peaks at 686.4 and 688.3 eV indicate covalent C-F bonds. This is related to the reaction between NH4HF and some of oxygen functional groups on CQDs with sp3 hybridized carbon atoms leads to formation of covalent C-F bonds. Also, due to low stability during the thermal process, some oxygenated groups tend to separate from CQDs surface and leave active sites with sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. These active sites then react with NH4HF under reaction conditions and form semi-ionic C-F bonds [60].

As it is seen in Fig. 7f, XPS binding energy of B 1 s peaks are recognized between 185 and 199 eV, identifying three different chemical states of boron. The main peak at 186 eV is assigned to the B-O band. The next peak located at 191 eV is allocated to B-C, B = C bonds and the B-F bond corresponds to the peak positioned at the binding energy of 197.6 eV The simultaneous doping of fluorine and boron in CQDs causes charge transfer in the sp2 carbon network and increases the electrical conductivity between the adjacent carbon atoms. Thus, the successful doping of boron and fluorine in carbon is confirmed by XPS analysis [58]. Fig. 7g shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ca 2p, which is deconvoluted as follows: (i) binding energy at 346.5 eV (Ca 2p3/2) is attributed to the characteristic bonds of calcium in carbonate due to air-adsorbed carbon species. The 2p3/2 peak is centered based on the adjustment of the C 1 s peak position (289.2 eV) and O 1 s (531.1 eV) and fitted to 346.5 eV for CaCO3. (ii) Peaks at 349.8 and 352.7 eV (Ca 2p1/2) may be attributed to formation of CaO particles by oxidation [61].

As shown in Fig. 7h, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of P 2p can be deconvoluted into three main peaks at 131.6, 133.4, and 135.3 eV, corresponding to P-C, P-C and P-O bonds. It is necessary to mention the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s at 285.4 eV corresponds to the C-P band. On the other hand, the functional groups at the edges of CQDs network can create P–O bonds [62]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum in the Mg region shows one main peak, which is related to Mg 2 s (Mg2+) at 88.06 eV, indicating MgO metal oxide is formed on the surface during the thermal synthesis reaction. (Fig. 7i) [63]. Finally, the peaks at 292.3 and 294.3 eV, which are separated by 2 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of K 2p (Fig. 7j), can be assigned to K 2p3/2 and K 2p1/2 of the K + ion and both of them refer to the K + state bound to anions such as oxide and fluoride. Therefore, theses peaks exhibit the presence of K2O and KF metal oxides in the CQDs sample [64].

To measure PL quantum yield (QY), number of emitted PL photons is divided by number of excited absorbed photons. QY values for synthesized pure and F,B co-doped CQDs have been estimated through comparing their PL intensity (at 350 nm excitation). Quinine sulfate indicating refractive index (η) of 1.33 in H2SO4 0.1 M (QY=0.54 by excitation at 350 nm) is chosen as a reference [65]. The QY is determined by the following equation, where ϕ, I, A, and η respectively represent QY, PL intensity, absorbance, and solution refractive index. Also, S subscript specifies CQD sample and the subscript R indicates reference. A QY=12.1% is measured for the pure CQDs while a satisfactory quantum yield of 40% is obtained for the 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs, confirming it is an excellent photocatalyst material.

The visible light photocatalytic RhB degradation over pure and F,B co-doped CQDs was done to explore how doping boron and fluorine elements in the photocatalysts structures affects their activities. Before doping the F and B elements in the CQD structure, the synthesis conditions (temperature and time) were optimized to find the best CQD with the highest photocatalytic RhB degradation activity. Therefore, grounded thymes were placed in the muffle furnace at different temperatures of 300, 400, and 500 °C for 3, 4, 5, and 10 h, and then subjected to photocatalytic degradation. Each experiment was started using CQDs under darkness without visible light irradiation, which displayed a slight decrease in RhB concentration by dye adsorption on photocatalyst pores and surface. Table 1 lists photocatalytic RhB degradation rates of all synthesized carbon quantum dots. Evidently, CQDs obtained from thyme at 400 °C for 5 h reveals the highest photocatalytic RhB degradation of 74%. Hence, it is chosen as the optimal photocatalyst and used for F and B co-doping in order to boost its photocatalytic capacity.

The selected optimal CQD was subjected to doping different percentages of boron and fluorine (1–1, 5–5, 7.5–7.5, 10–10, 12.5–12.5, and 15%−15%) to attain the best photocatalyst, and their RhB photocatalytic destruction rates are obtained to be 71%, 89.6%, 81.8%, 91.7%, 73.7% and 86.1%, respectively, during 180 min of visible light irradiation.

Table 1 illustrates RhB photocatalytic degradation efficiencies in water using un-doped and F,B co-doped CQDs. Obviously, un-doped CQD degrades 74% of RhB after 180 min visible light radiation whereas 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD exhibits the highest photocatalytic activities of 89.13% and 86.15% in 60 and 40 min, respectively. Thus, the ideal photocatalyst would be10%F-10%B co-doped CQD, in which boron and fluorine doping within carbon materials enhance photocatalytic activities through diminishing recombination of electrons with holes.

Pphotocatalyst dosage influence was explored onto photocatalytic RhB degradation efficacy using different dosages of 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD optimal sample (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25 g/L) which were applied for degradation of 5 ppm RhB pollutant. It is found that with increase of the catalyst dose, the degradation rate also increases (Fig. 8 a), which can be related to the increase of catalytic active sites and, as a result, extra RhB molecules can be loaded over catalyst. 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD has a high adsorption ability during 60 min of darkness, and then when the lamp is turned on, it begins to photocatalytically degrade the pollutant released into the environment after adsorption. Thus, by increasing the initial amount of the catalyst, the adsorption in the dark increases significantly, but the power of photocatalytic degradation does not increase to this extent. For example, the catalyst dose of 0.5 g/L, which is chosen as the optimum value, has about 80% of the initial adsorption in the dark, and the photocatalytic decompositions are 89.13% and 86.15% in 60 and 40 min, respectively, after irradiation. However, the catalyst amount of 1.25 g/L, which is 2.5 times the optimal catalyst dosage (which is not economically suitable), shows initial adsorption of 93%, and the photocatalytic degradation rates of 97.7% and 96.7% in 60 and 40 min of radiation, respectively. Fig. 8b exhibits decrease in UV−Vis absorbance peak intensity of RhB by time due to RhB degradation over optimum F,B co-doped CQDs. Various first-order reaction kinetics of RhB degradation using diverse doses of 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD are observed in Fig. 8c. First-order rate constant values for 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0, and 1.25 g/L of 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD are respectively 0.0109, 0.00602, 0.00275, 0.0055, and 0.00818 min−1. Fig. 8d reveals degradation performances of diverse doses of 10%F-10%B co-doped carbon photocatalyst nanoparticles.

To figure out pollutant concentration influence onto photocatalytic performance of the optimal 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD photocatalyst (0.50 g/L), varied RhB concentrations (5, 7, 9, 11 mg/L) were tested. Fig. 9 a proves that lower photocatalytic degradation rates are measured via increasing RhB concentration. In these experiments, a constant photocatalyst dosage is used, which means catalytic active sites density is constant. Therefore, when RhB adsorption over photocatalyst surface increases, more active sites are populated, less active radicals (•OH, •O2 −, •OOH, and holes) are generated and RhB photocatalytic degradation efficacy diminishes. Besides, at higher RhB concentrations, solution becomes more turbid and thereby less visible light photons reach catalytic reactive sites that decrease reaction performance. Fig. 9b displays that RhB absorption intensity decreases by time signifying higher degradation upon longer visible light radiation. Also, plots of reaction kinetics for degradation of diverse RhB concentrations using 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD photocatalyst are revealed in Fig. 9c. Based on these plots, rate constants for degrading 5, 7, 9, 11 ppm of RhB are respectively 0.00602, 0.00287, 0.00114 and 0.00161 min−1. Fig. 9d illustrates RhB degradation rates are 89.13, 77.27, 48.65, and 41.54%, respectively, for 5, 7, 9, and 11 ppm RhB concentrations over optimal 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD (0.50 g/L) after 60 min.

Photocatalytic RhB degradation is extremely influenced by pH of solution. Therefore, pH was changed from 3 to 4, 5, 7, and 9 to examine its effect on photocatalyst performance. Synthesized 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD (0.50 g/L) shows superlative RhB photocatalytic degradation of at pH 5, which is equivalent to 89.13% degradation. Consequently, at pH<5, photocatalyst surface is positively charged but at pH>5, it is negatively charged.

There are two −NR2 amino and one −COOH carboxylic acid groups in RhB structure. Accordingly, it is positively charged at pH<7 because of −NR2 protonation, whereas it is negatively charged at pH>7 via −COOH deprotonation. Thus, maximum RhB adsorption over 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD occurs if pH varies between 5 and 7. Lower photocatalytic degradation performance is measured at pH<5 (very acidic environments) due to electrostatic repulsions happening among cationic RhB molecule and 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD. Also, extensively less active sites exist onto photocatalyst surface at pH>5, which decline reaction rate owing to highly robust electrostatic attractions among cationic RhB molecule and 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD with negative surface charge.

In fact, according to Eqs. (5)-(9), formation of hydroxyl radicals is very dependent to pH [66]. The pKa of •OOH/•O2 − is 4.88. Hence, formation of •OH radicals only happens if positive holes react with H2O when pKa<pH, see Eqs. (5)-(9).

Fig. 10 a exhibits RhB photocatalytic degradation kinetics over 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD (0.5 g/L) at diverse pH levels that can be used to obtain first-order rate constants (Fig. 10b). When varied pH levels of 3, 4, 5, 7, and 9 are used, RhB degradation rates after 180 min visible light radiation are 82, 100, 99, 96.5, and 91.4%, respectively (see Fig. 10c). As a result, pH=5 is the optimal value, at which maximum RhB degradation is measured.

Using historical data design, the results of experiments conducted under various conditions were used as the input parameters, and their related predicted responses were obtained utilizing Design Expert 7 software. Table 2 presents designed experiments according to historical data design. Four critical variables have been considered which include initial dye concentration (A), catalyst dosage (B), pH (C), and radiation time (D). Also, dual interactions of these variables have been modelled. It was found that quadratic model best matched to the degradation rate of RhB (Y) using the 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD optimal photocatalyst, which afforded empirical formula given by Eq. (10), depending on A, B, C, and D parameters. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) in addition to Fisher test were performed to find if this model is statistically significant. Fig. S4 illustrates that there is a good relationship between predicted versus actual values of RhB photocatalytic degradation using 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs under visible light radiation.

Influences of variables and their interactions onto RhB degradation rates over the optimum 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD photocatalyst were explored using both of two- and the three -dimensional contour and surface diagrams, respectively. Such plots simultaneously present two factors effects onto RhB degradation rate as the response while other parameters are kept constant.

Fig. 11 a exhibits the interactions of pollutant concentration and photocatalyst dosage and their influences onto RhB degradation efficiency under constant radiation time of 60 min. With the increase of photocatalyst amount from 0.25 to 1.25 g/L, RhB degradation efficiency also increases, which can be related to the rise of active catalytic sites. As a result, extra RhB can be loaded onto catalyst. In contrast, with the increase in the pollutant concentration (5, 7, 9, and 11 ppm), the active sites and then the pollutant adsorption on the catalyst surface will be decreased, and conversely, with the increasing pollutant concentration, the reaction medium turbidity will prevent light from reaching the catalyst and the degradation percentage decreases.

As it is clear, the effects of pollutant concentration and pH are provided in Fig. 11b. Rate of degradation decreases by the enhancement of pollutant concentration, and with the rise in pH from 3 to 4 and 5, the rate of degradation increases firstly, but further increase of pH from 7 to 9 causes the percentage of RhB dye degradation to decrease. Fig. 11c exhibits that degradation rate increases upon increasing radiation time, while RhB degradation rate shows a downward trend with increasing concentration of the pollutant due to the reasons mentioned before.

Fig. 11d represents the interplay between catalyst dosage and pH (3, 4, 5, 7, and 9), which illustrates the maximum surface adsorption of the dye on the catalyst takes place between pH 5–7. On the other hand, at a pH above 7, in addition to reducing the pollutant adsorption on the surface of the photocatalyst, the accumulation of dye particles also increases, which leads to a decrease in photocatalytic degradation at pH 9. In this graph, the degradation increases and decreases with increasing catalyst dosage and pH.

Fig. 11e presents mutual influences of catalyst dosage and radiation time onto RhB degradation efficiency. With increasing the catalyst dose, the maximum percentage of destruction occurs 40 min after irradiation and remains approximately constant from 40 to 60 min. Finally, in Fig. 11f, the interaction between irradiation time and pH is mentioned as follows: both are almost equally effective in photocatalytic degradation, and the role of increasing the irradiation time is slightly greater than increasing the pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Turn over number and turn over frequency
ce:section.ce:para:
When estimating activity of a heterogeneous/homogeneous catalyst, turn over number (TON) can be measured because it acts a central role on catalyst stability. TON is dimensionless that is equal to moles of product divided by catalytic sites density before deactivating in specific process condition. Regardless of the reaction time, the catalyst can reach 100% performance before becoming deactivated [67]. Thus, TON indicates highest product yielded using an active catalyst. The TON of the RhB degradation catalyst is calculated using Eq. (11):

of
RhB
degraded
Number

of
CQD
catalyst

The turnover frequency (TOF) is a measure of the instantaneous performance or efficiency of the catalyst. Also, to assume the instant efficiency of the catalyst, the TOF is calculated using TON divided by photocatalytic RhB decomposition time according to Eq. (12) [67]. The TON and TOF values in Table 3 display that the largest values of 2.16 × 10−4 and 5.40×10−6 min−1, respectively, are measured for the 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD optimal photocatalyst, which illustrates the highest photocatalytic efficiency. However, the lowest TON of 1.69×10−4 and TOF of 4.22×10−6 min−1 are attained for the pure CQD photocatalyst, confirming the F and B co-doping highly boosted the photocatalytic RhB degradation performance.

TON
Reaction

Important principles of using semiconductors as photocatalysts are generation, separation, plus transport of light induced electrons and holes onto photocatalyst surface to proceed reaction at catalyst surface. Photoelectrochemical techniques provide precious data on such phenomena. EIS spectra and transient photocurrent response analyses were performed using electrodes based on pure and 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs to figure out synergetic influences of doping boron and fluorine elements onto electrochemical behaviors, as can be seen in Fig. 12 . In these cases, the radius of EIS semicircle exhibits charge transport at interface of electrolyte and photocatalyst [68]. It is shown in Fig. 12a that a highly smaller radius of semicircle is obtained for 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD compared to pure CQD, indicating a lower charge transport resistance in the former but a larger one in the latter. This is related to boron and fluorine doping, less electron-hole recombination and superior separation of charge carriers.

Fig. 12b exhibits that the photocurrent transient responses of electrodes based on pure and 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs are obtained during multiple alternating radiations (on and off cycles) at their open circuit voltages. It must be mentioned that the optical current density of 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs is superior to un-doped CQDs due to smaller bandgap, higher light absorption, and superior separation of charges. This means that there is less electron-hole recombination and longer lifetimes are predicted for the charge carriers, which are consistent with the PL results. Moreover, it is clear that the response of the photocatalyst is very fast. Overall, resistance to charge transport has been declined via doping electron withdrawing F as well as almost electron donating B elements in CQDs.

All of structural, photocatalytic, and photoelectrochemical characteristics verified maximum photocatalytic performance for 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD with highest visible light collection capacity that is due to quantum dot size and the lowest electron-hole recombination rate. In fact, the scattered light is captured by photocatalyst particles. An effective separation of electron from hole boosts photocatalytic performance. A photocatalytic reaction is initiated by the generation of holes (h +) and electrons (e) while the carbon catalyst is illuminated with visible light. Then, photogenerated holes, superoxide (•O2 −), and hydroxyl (•OH) radicals as three foremost reactive species, are produced via advanced oxidation processes as shown in Eqs. (13)-(15), which may occur by the reactions of electrons and holes with water and O2 molecules to photocatalytically degrade surrounding organic pollutant molecules. Because the holes react with the adsorbed water molecules, •OH radicals are quickly formed. Conversely, reaction of electrons created by light and oxygen molecules forms superoxide radicals. All h +, e, •O2 −, and •OH species are involved in the degradation of RhB so that the solution color changes from pink to colorless.

CQD electronic structure is modified upon chemical doping with fluorine and boron elements. A reason for this effect is that co-doped F and B elements diminutions photocatalyst's band gap and leads to extra light generated charges that boost photocatalytic efficiency. Hence, the co-doped of F and B elements apparently further inhibit recombination of electrons with holes, supported with photoelectrochemical results and the PL spectra, that satisfactorily enhance photocatalytic property.

Photocatalytic RhB degradation mechanism using 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs has been carefully studied to identify reactive species that are created in this reaction as radicals and holes. For this purpose, some capturing tests were performed using methanol, EDTA, and benzoquinone (BQ) (5 mmol of each scavenger) that are respectively •OH, hole, and •O2 − scavengers. Reaction rates are lowered by adding EDTA (hole scavenger), methanol (hydroxyl radical scavenger) and BQ (superoxide ion scavenger) such that methanol decreases the degradation efficiency to the utmost extent, see Fig. 11c. Hence, these scavengers reveal RhB photocatalytic degradation rates of 72.6% for EDTA, 65.1% for methanol, and 77.2% for BQ. From this finding, •OH radical plays the most crucial role among •O2 − radical and holes. Therefore, •O2 − and hole do not have key effects in RhB photocatalytic oxidation on 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs. Based on the above results, the •OH radical is the predominantly active species responsible for visible light photocatalytic RhB degradation.

Based on Mott-Schottky analysis, band positions of pure and 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs semiconductor photocatalysts were evaluated. EIS spectra were taken under dark. The associated flat band potential (Vfb) values are obtained by the Mott-Schottky, Eq. Mott-Schottky diagrams illustrate positive slopes (Figs. 13a-13b) for pure and 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs, confirming they are n-type semiconductors [69]. The Fermi levels for pure CDs and 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs are acquired to be respectively +0.45 V and −0.26 V against Ag/AgCl, through extrapolating of Mott-Schottky diagrams. To convert these potential values versus Ag/AgCl into potentials versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE), Eq. (17) was used.
(17)

Using the potentials versus NHE, Efb amounts are obtained to be respectively +0.65 V and −0.06 V. Typically, Efb gained from Mott-Schottky analysis equals EF in semiconductor. Moreover, the CB level of an n-type semiconductor is 0.1 V higher than its Fermi level (EF). Consequently, conduction band bottoms of pure and 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs are respectively +0.55 V and −0.16 V.

Top of the valence band potential is also calculated according to Eq. (18). Therefore, superoxide radicals cannot be produced from pure CQDs, and only the reaction of photogenerated electrons from 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs with O2 generates •O2 − radical onto surface of catalyst (Eq. (19)), As can be seen in Fig. 13c.

A most possible reason for central role of hydroxyl radical in RhB photocatalytic degradation by CQDs is probably due to the fact that •OH/OH‒ redox potential (+1.99 versus NHE, pH 7) is below valence band energies of 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs (+2.09 versus NHE, pH 7) and pure CQDs (+3.11 versus NHE, pH 7). Accordingly, light produced holes in these two photocatalyst materials oxidize OH‒ into •OH radical (Eq. (20)). Nonetheless, •O2 −/O2 redox potential (–0.13 V versus NHE, pH 7) is upper compared to conduction band of 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs (−0.16 vs NHE) and in contrast, it is below pure CQDs energy (+0.55 vs NHE).

Meanwhile, the hydroxyl radical is also formed from the following equation, which is why the photocatalytic efficiency of 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs is higher than that of pure CQDs (Eq. (21)).

By paying attention to the elemental nature of boron and fluorine elements, it can be stated that B atom configuration is 2s2 2p1. After B doping into CQDs, it forms sp2 hybridized orbitals due to formation of C-B bonds with three neighboring C atoms. The dopant distorts CQD slightly so that all of B-C bond lengths are 1.49 Å (CC bond length is 1.43 Å) [70].

To study charge transfer and electronic structure in B-doped graphene, M Wu et al. performed DOS calculations [70]. The graphene honeycomb planar structure was best maintained upon B doping, hence linear energy dispersion close to Dirac point was not completely varied. Boron doping was excellently viewed as hole doping due to EF moved 0.7 eV lower than Dirac point. In previous works by S.Agnoli et al. experiments [71] and M. Wu et al. [70], B-doped graphene indicated p-type semiconductivity. This was expected since boron atoms lacked electrons compared to carbon atoms. Besides, they also performed a Baader charge analysis that showed 1.84 electron transport from B towards C atom. Such observation was related to more electronegativity of C compared to B atoms.

Fluorine doping is a completely different case. The electron configuration of fluorine (2s2 2p5) is restricted. Fluorine atoms are sp3 hybridized and can only bind to one of the adjacent carbon atoms. From graphene, it gains 0.62 electrons, according to Bader charge analysis, because the F atom of doped graphene is only bound to one carbon atom. F-doped graphene indicates n-type semiconductivity, as is the case for N- and O-doped graphene. Electronegativity balance principle can be deliberated to predict how Fermi level changes upon F, O, N, and B doping. When electropositive B binds to neighboring C atoms of graphene, electron flows from carbon atoms to B atoms and the semiconductor becomes a p-type material. Also, density of states beneath bandgap enhances and correspondingly Fermi level of graphene is reduced. As carbon atoms create bonds with electronegative F, N, and O elements in the graphene sheet, electrons transfer from carbons towards the F, N, and O elements, and n-type semiconductors are formed [70]. Hence, density of states increases beyond bandgap based on Fermi formula. Energy of the Fermi level of graphene also rises.

According to Mott-Schottky electrochemical analysis, the B- and F-doped CQDs simultaneously showed n- and p-type semiconductivity. In this case, n-conductivity is originated from the incorporation of fluorine atoms and conductivity p by boron atoms inside and on the CQD framework and surface. The inherent sp2 bonds of CQD serve as a connection between n and p zones that create integrated p–n diodes and internal Z-scheme charge transport within CQD structure, like the one observed in natural photosynthesis [1]. Consequently, conduction band electrons in the p-type domain can react with oxygen and produce superoxide radicals, while the valence band holes in the n-type domain can oxidize water or hydroxide ions and convert them into hydroxyl radicals. On the other hand, the n-type conduction band electrons are directly combined with the valence band holes of the p-type domain and a Z-shaped pattern is designed (Fig. 14 ).

Herein, 2.2% B doping in CQDs causes p-type semiconductivity and 7.3% F-doped element has formed an n-type zone in the semiconductor structure. When RhB is completely fragmented, its color changes from dark pink to almost colorless. The possible mechanism shown in Fig. S5 includes the following steps [72]. (1) an intermediate product is formed due to N-de-ethylation, (2) the chromophore is cleaved, (3) the ring is opened, and (4) non-toxic mineral products are formed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability of optimal photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic stability of the optimal 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs photocatalyst (0.5 g/L) was studied in four consecutive runs of the photocatalytic RhB degradation reactions over 60 min at a constant pH of 5 and an initial RhB concentration of 5 mg/L. After each cycle, the photocatalyst was rinsed with distilled water and ethanol and then dried for reuse. As depicted in Fig. S6(a), the efficiency decreased from 89% in the first cycle to 78.9% in the fourth cycle. It can be seen that the photocatalytic activity after four times reusing the optimal photocatalyst has not changed significantly, and almost 10% of its photocatalytic activity has decreased. Therefore, the 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs sample shows a good stability up to its fourth cycle. Additionally, the stability of the 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs photocatalyst has been investigated before and after the photocatalytic reaction using XRD and FTIR analysis techniques, as shown in Figs. S6(b) and S6(c). The XRD patterns reveals that despite small and minor changes in the structure of the photocatalyst, which are expected, the photocatalytic activity has not changed considerably. This minor changes in the structure have led to the creation of some sharp peaks in the XRD pattern and additional bands in the FTIR spectrum of the reused photocatalyst.

Even though the catalyst was washed several times with water and ethanol, there was probably very slight chemisorption of impurities in the holes, which caused a little change in the structure of the photocatalyst, resulting in small changes in the XRD pattern. Furthermore, there are changes in the intensity of the IR bands so that increased intensity of the peaks is observed especially in the region 500–1200 cm−1. A sharp peak of 2354 cm−1 is also due to the absorption of carbon dioxide from air. It seems that the surface of the catalyst has changed in terms of porosity and surface functional groups due to its interactions with the molecules existing in the solution, which include surface absorption of the RhB reactant molecules or even degradation products. Thus, the surface of the photocatalyst has undergone a minor change, which has been confirmed by XRD and FTIR analysis.

A facile and one-step calcination technique has been introduced for green, super cheap, and in-situ synthesis of CQDs from a natural plant source called garden thyme. The simultaneous fluorine and boron doping strategy was successfully done to modify CQDs electronic characteristics. Optimal 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs exhibited 40% quantum yield efficiency. The particle size <10 nm were measured by HR-TEM micrographs for the CQDs with semi-spherical morphology. The increase in photocatalytic activity of doped carbon dots was investigated through RhB degradation in water in visible light radiation. In addition, loading of fluorine and boron with different percentages into CQDs structure was carried out and 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD was selected as the optimal photocatalyst, which had the highest degradation efficiency of 86.15% under 40 min of visible light radiation. Notably, fluorine and boron weight percentages were key factors in increasing the quantum efficiency so that the optimal 10%F-10%B co-doped CQD photocatalyst illustrated the highest efficiency. The first-order kinetic rate constant was 3.5 times bigger than the rate constant of undoped CQDs. Photoelectrochemical measurements including light current response, EIS Niquist, and Matt Schottky graphs showed that doping CQDs with fluorine and boron increased electron conductivity and electron storage capacity via effectively boosting charge transport and mobility as an electron storage center. Furthermore, holes, •OH, and superoxide radicals were participated in reaction, and the role of hydroxyl radical was the most prominent among others. On the other hand, the internal design of the Z-Scheme structure, due to having both p-type and n-type domains, greatly improved the photo-induced electron-hole separation. It fact, 2.2% boron doping in CQDs caused a p-type conductivity while 7.3% F doping endowed with an n-type semiconductivity to the structure, which totally formed an internal Z-scheme heterojunction within the 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs. Of course, the intelligent choice of precursors and how to synthesize CQDs can be very important in discovering new and highly efficient CQDs with exceptional properties that can be used as a strong tool to address various issues related to the environment and energy as two of the biggest challenges in today's modern society. In summary, the 10%F-10%B co-doped CQDs optimal photocatalysts designed and synthesized from garden thyme as a biomass source could find extensive applications in water treatment.
The data associated with this work will be delivered on request.

Zahra Mirzaeifard: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Zahra Shariatinia: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft.
